name: Suzutani
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima (Hyogo). Sire Tanishige FB211; dam Suzunami FB227 (by Shigeshigenami FB219); maternal granddam Suzuko (by Yasuchiyo Doi). With Rikitani, one of the first 100% Tajima females ever to leave Japan.
importer: Mannett Group (later World K's)
notable_offspring: Dam of the two most important 2nd-generation sires outside Japan — Shigeshigetani and Sanjirou — plus Kanadagene 100D, Suzuharu FB5769 and Suzumichi FB13047. The Shigeshigetani sire line descends from her.
marbling_note: Regarded as the greatest Tajima and most influential female ever exported; a very prolific flush cow whose Tajima genetics are a cornerstone of marbling quality in fullblood herds worldwide.
bio: Suzutani is widely called the most famous and genetically influential Wagyu cow ever to leave Japan. A 100% Tajima female born December 28, 1991, she was sired by Tanishige (FB211) and out of Suzunami (FB227), a daughter of the legendary Tajima sire Shigeshigenami. In 1993 the Mannett Group (which became World K's) imported her alongside Rikitani and Okutani plus the bulls Michifuku and Haruki II — the shipment that founded fullblood Wagyu in North America. Unlike her less fertile shipmate Rikitani, Suzutani proved an exceptionally prolific flush cow. She produced Shigeshigetani and Sanjirou, arguably the two most consequential second-generation sires bred outside Japan, and the Shigeshigetani line traces directly to her. Additional offspring extended her reach into Canada. Because so many elite modern fullbloods carry her blood, she remains a benchmark maternal name in Wagyu pedigrees and a symbol of concentrated Tajima marbling genetics.
photo_note: Foundation cows are rarely photographed; no verified image located.
notes: Registration FB1617 and pedigree confirmed via WSU Foundation Wagyu Parentage document (Rocking 711) and Wagyu World.

name: Rikitani
bloodline_detail: 100% Tajima. Sire Tanishige FB211; dam Shigekane FB230 (by Shigeshigenami); maternal granddam Nishino 179. With Suzutani, among the first 100% Tajima females exported.
importer: Mannett Group (later World K's)
notable_offspring: Son Rikihari/Rikiharu (FB10973) was the first fullblood Wagyu calf born outside Japan (June 19, 1994, Canada), sired by Haruki II.
marbling_note: Not a prolific flush cow and left few progeny, but her descendants are noted for exceptional marbling scores.
bio: Rikitani was one of the three near-100% Tajima foundation females imported by the Mannett Group in 1993, the shipment that established fullblood Wagyu in North America. Born January 13, 1992, she was a pure Tajima female sired by Tanishige (FB211) and out of Shigekane (FB230), a daughter of the great Tajima sire Shigeshigenami — the shared ancestor that made all three 1993 heifers so prized. Together with Suzutani she was among the very first 100% Tajima females ever to leave Japan. Her enduring place in history comes from her son Rikihari (also rendered Rikiharu, FB10973), sired by Haruki II, who on June 19, 1994 in Canada became the first fullblood Wagyu calf born anywhere outside Japan. Rikitani herself proved a poor flush cow and produced comparatively few offspring, so her direct genetic footprint is narrower than Suzutani's or Okutani's, but her progeny are prized for elite marbling and her name anchors the earliest chapter of the breed's export history.
notes: FB1618 and pedigree from WSU Parentage document and Wagyu World; calf name appears as both Rikihari and Rikiharu (FB10973).

name: Okutani
bloodline_detail: ~75% Tajima / 25% Fujiyoshi (Shimane). Sire Okushige FB218 (No.1 for BMS in Japan 1990); dam Fujitani FB223 (by Kikutani 908); maternal granddam Itofuji 9 (by Dai 7 Itozakura). Considered the strongest sire stack of the 1993 trio.
importer: Mannett Group (later World K's)
notable_offspring: Embryo dam of Fujiko (FB1984) — the first fullblood Wagyu born in the USA (June 24, 1994, by Haruki II) — plus Beijiro, Genjiro and Okuharu. Fujiko's cloning rights sold for ~$25,000 in 2012.
marbling_note: Carried an elite concentration of top BMS sires (Okushige, Kikutani, Dai 7 Itozakura, Shigeshigenami); regarded as equally influential to Suzutani despite her early death.
bio: Okutani was the third of the Mannett Group's landmark 1993 foundation females and, on pedigree, arguably the most powerfully bred of the trio. Born January 5, 1992, she was roughly 75% Tajima and 25% Fujiyoshi (Shimane), sired by Okushige (FB218) — the bull ranked No. 1 for marbling in Japan in 1990 — and out of Fujitani (FB223), a daughter of the elite sire Kikutani 908, with Dai 7 Itozakura appearing through her granddam. This stacked her pedigree with several of the greatest marbling sires in Japanese history. Tragically she died soon after arriving in the United States, so few live progeny exist, but embryos recovered from her changed the breed: on June 24, 1994 she produced Fujiko (FB1984), the first fullblood Wagyu ever born in the USA, sired by Haruki II. Fujiko became one of the breed's most influential and defect-free females, her cloning rights fetching about $25,000 in 2012. Through Fujiko, Okutani's genetics remain foundational across American fullblood herds.
notes: FB1616, birth date and pedigree from Wagyu World; bloodline split and offspring cross-checked with Reserve Cattle Co. and Rocking 711.

name: Okahana
bloodline_detail: Precise strain composition not documented. Part of the Mannett Group's second (1994) black shipment.
importer: Mannett Group (later World K's)
notable_offspring: Founder of the 'Okahana' maternal line still cited in modern pedigrees.
bio: Okahana was one of four black foundation females — with Nakayuki, Kanetani and Nakagishi 5 — imported from Japan by the Mannett Group (World K's) in August 1994, a shipment that also brought the bulls Kenhanafuji and Takazakura. This second consignment broadened the maternal base of the young North American fullblood herd beyond the three celebrated 1993 cows. Calves registered from the group after arrival included Tanitsuru, Nakazakura, Kitaguni Jr and Reiko. Okahana's specific pedigree and registration number are not well documented in public breeder sources, but her name survives as a recognized maternal line in later pedigrees. She matters as part of the small nucleus of genuinely Japan-exported foundation females on which the entire fullblood population outside Japan was built.
notes: Import event well documented; registration, birth year, sire and dam not found — left null.

importer: Mannett Group (later World K's)

Post-arrival calves from the group included Tanitsuru, Nakazakura, Kitaguni Jr and Reiko. name: Nakagishi 5
bloodline_detail: Pedigree reported to trace to Dai 7 (Dai Nana) Itozakura and to Harumi on both sire and dam sides — strong Itozakura/Fujiyoshi (Shimane) influence rather than pure Tajima.
importer: Mannett Group (later World K's)
marbling_note: Valued for the Itozakura influence in her pedigree, a line associated with frame and size as well as marbling.
bio: Nakagishi 5 was one of the four black foundation females imported from Japan by the Mannett Group (World K's) in August 1994, alongside Okahana, Nakayuki and Kanetani plus the bulls Kenhanafuji and Takazakura. She is the best-characterized of that group's dams: her pedigree is reported to carry the great sire Dai 7 Itozakura together with Harumi appearing on both the sire and dam sides, giving her a distinctly Itozakura/Shimane-influenced makeup rather than the pure Tajima of the 1993 cows. This made her useful for adding line diversity to a narrow founding gene pool. Registered calves from the 1994 group after arrival included Tanitsuru, Nakazakura, Kitaguni Jr and Reiko. Her exact registration number and birth year were not found in public sources. She matters as a verifiable Japan-exported foundation dam contributing non-Tajima strength to the breed's maternal base outside Japan.
notes: Dai 7 Itozakura/Harumi pedigree note from a single breeder-summary source.

name: Namiko
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto Prefecture. One of the nine red foundation females in the 1994 Wood importation and one of the seven purchased for HeartBrand.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (with Yukio Kurosawatsu) → HeartBrand Ranch
marbling_note: Akaushi are prized for healthy monounsaturated (oleic) fat; the HeartBrand foundation dams are the genetic base of essentially all registered fullblood Akaushi outside Japan.
bio: Namiko was one of nine red (Akaushi) foundation females in the pivotal 1994 importation led by Dr. Al and Marie Wood, working with Mr. Yukio Kurosawatsu out of Kumamoto Prefecture — the shipment that created the entire fullblood Akaushi population outside Japan. She was among the seven females (with Akiko, Haruko, Fuyuko, Ume, Dai 8 Marunami and Ringo 117) subsequently acquired for HeartBrand Ranch in Harwood, Texas, alongside the bulls Shigemaru, Tamamaru and Hikari. The selection was made in Japan by Bruce Hemingson, who chose the foundation cattle firsthand; the first fullblood Akaushi born on American soil, Big Al, came from her shipmate Akiko, bred to Dai 10 Mitsumaru in quarantine. Individual pedigree detail for Namiko is not published in the sources reviewed. Her significance is collective: as one of a tiny closed founder set, she is an irreplaceable pillar of the Akaushi breed abroad, whose descendants are registered with both the American Wagyu Association and the Akaushi Association of America.
notes: Import event, Kumamoto origin and HeartBrand routing well attested; individual pedigree/registration not found.

name: Akiko
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females; one of the seven HeartBrand founders. Distinct from the US-born BLACK cow 'Akiko' FB9499 (a Suzutani daughter).
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
notable_offspring: Dam of Big Al (registered Mitsuaki), sired by Dai 10 Mitsumaru — conceived in Japan's quarantine and born in America as the first fullblood Akaushi on American soil.
marbling_note: Foundation of the HeartBrand Akaushi program known for high oleic-acid, health-oriented marbling.
bio: Akiko is the Akaushi cow behind one of the great origin stories of American Wagyu — an account preserved firsthand by Bruce Hemingson, who selected the foundation cattle in Japan. One of the best red females chosen for Dr. Al Wood (a heart surgeon) and his wife Marie, she came up open — not pregnant — during the roughly six-month export quarantine every animal had to pass in Japan. In a founding group of only a handful of cows, losing a season from one of the best was unthinkable. But getting her bred was nearly impossible: her sire line and the elite Akaushi genetics traced to Kumamoto on the island of Kyushu, and cattle and semen could not simply be moved across Japan's prefecture lines. So, by Hemingson's account, they arranged to meet a man in a grocery-store parking lot in Japan and paid $5,000 cash for a single straw of the elite sire Dai 10 Mitsumaru. Then they slipped into the quarantine facility after dark; with no chute available, they caught Akiko and held her pinned against the wall while a vet artificially inseminated her by lantern-light. It worked. Akiko arrived in America pregnant and gave birth here to Big Al (registered Mitsuaki) — the first fullblood Akaushi born on American soil and a cornerstone sire of the HeartBrand program. She should not be confused with the later US-born black cow 'Akiko' (FB9499), a Suzutani daughter; the two share only a name. Few foundation females carry a legacy as vivid, or as consequential, as hers.
notes: Big Al story per Bruce Hemingson (firsthand participant). CORRECTED: bred in Japan's quarantine, not the US — Akiko arrived pregnant and calved in America. Sire of Big Al = Dai 10 Mitsumaru. Distinct from black cow Akiko FB9499.

name: Haruko
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females; one of the seven HeartBrand founders.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood → HeartBrand Ranch
marbling_note: Part of the closed founder set behind HeartBrand/registered Akaushi and its high monounsaturated-fat reputation.
bio: Haruko was one of nine red Akaushi foundation females imported from Kumamoto Prefecture in 1994 by Dr. Al and Marie Wood, and one of the seven subsequently purchased to establish HeartBrand Ranch in Harwood, Texas, together with Namiko, Akiko, Fuyuko, Ume, Dai 8 Marunami and Ringo 117 and the bulls Shigemaru, Tamamaru and Hikari. This single importation is the genetic wellspring of virtually all fullblood Akaushi outside Japan. The heifers were bred to elite Japanese sires Namimaru and Dai 10 Mitsumaru before export. Haruko's specific pedigree is not documented in the public sources reviewed. She matters as one of only a handful of authentic red foundation dams from which the entire Akaushi maternal base abroad descends.
notes: Founder status well attested; individual pedigree/registration not found.

bio: Fuyuko was one of the nine red Akaushi foundation females in Dr. Al and Marie Wood's 1994 importation from Kumamoto Prefecture and one of the seven acquired to found HeartBrand Ranch in Harwood, Texas. Her cohort — bred before export to the top Japanese sires Namimaru and Dai 10 Mitsumaru — represents the complete and closed founding gene pool of fullblood Akaushi outside Japan, now registered through both the American Wagyu Association and the Akaushi Association of America. Individual pedigree records for Fuyuko were not found in public sources. She is significant as one of the small nucleus of genuine red foundation dams behind every registered Akaushi abroad.
notes: Founder status well attested; individual pedigree/registration not found.

name: Namoi
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. One of the nine 1994 Wood red foundation females. Routed to Canada (Wagyu Sekai) rather than HeartBrand.
importer: Dr. Al & Marie Wood (import); acquired by Wagyu Sekai (Canada)
notable_offspring: Taken to Canada with her calf; part of the Canadian Akaushi foundation.
bio: Namoi — also rendered Naomi — was one of nine red Akaushi foundation females in Dr. Al and Marie Wood's 1994 importation from Kumamoto Prefecture. Rather than joining the HeartBrand nucleus in Texas, she and her calf were taken to Canada by Wagyu Sekai (Yukio and Ken Kurosawatsu), together with Dai 3 Namiaki, helping seed the Canadian Akaushi foundation. Her specific pedigree is not documented in the sources reviewed. She matters as one of the very small set of authentic red foundation dams exported before the 1997 Japanese ban, and as a bridge of Akaushi genetics into Canada.
notes: Spelled Namoi and Naomi across sources. Canadian routing via Wagyu Sekai from Akaushi Insight.

name: Ringo 117
bloodline_detail: Japanese Red / Akaushi from Kumamoto. Born in Japan in 1994 as the natural calf of foundation dam Dai 8 Marunami, and imported with her in the Wood shipment.

bio: Ringo 117 was born in Japan in 1994, the natural calf of the foundation Akaushi dam Dai 8 Marunami, and came to America with her in Dr. Al and Marie Wood's Kumamoto importation — one of the nine females and three bulls that founded HeartBrand and the entire fullblood Akaushi population outside Japan. As a young imported female she is counted among the HeartBrand foundation dams. (An earlier ambiguity in some accounts — whether she was imported or first-generation US-born — is resolved by her documented 1994 Japanese birth to Dai 8 Marunami.) Further individual pedigree detail is not published in the sources reviewed, but her place among the small closed group of genuine Akaushi founders is secure.
notes: Resolved: Ringo 117 born Japan 1994, natural calf of Dai 8 Marunami, imported with her dam (per Beyond Beef / Akaushi Genetics).

Individual pedigree detail for Taguchi 9 is not published in the sources reviewed. She matters as one of the last verifiable Japan-exported foundation females before the 1997 ban.

bio: Yuriko 2 completes the Japanese Venture Partners female roster. JVP's 1994 importation is repeatedly described in the breed histories as ten fullblood black females plus two red heifers, led by the Tajima bulls Fukutsuru 068, Kikuyasu-400 and Yasutanisakura. Rocking 711's chronological list names both Yuriko 1 and Yuriko 2 among the ten black cows, alongside Chisahime 662, Chiyofuku 992, Fukutomi 990, Kikuhana 298, Shigehime 208, Tokuhime 486, Yasufuji 1/4 and Yoshifuku 2. Yuriko 2 is the tenth and was missing from the registry. Her individual pedigree, registration and birth year are not published in the accessible sources. bio: Hatsuko is one of the named foundation dams from Chris Walker's Westholme importation, the largest single infusion of Japanese female genetics ever to leave Japan. Westholme brought 25 black females from ET Japan Company in Hokkaido in 1997 and a further 59 in 1998 (about 84 total), together with three founding bulls: Hirashige-Tayasu (Kedaka), Itomoritaka (Fujiyoshi) and Kitateruyasu Doi (Tajiri). Uniquely, the consignment emphasised Kedaka and Fujiyoshi strains for milk and size rather than pure Tajima. Hatsuko is documented among the dams that bred on in Australia, and the herd she helped found is now the backbone of the Australian Agricultural Company's Westholme brand. Her individual pedigree is not published in the accessible sources.
notes: Named among Westholme breeding dams by Wagyu International and Rocking 711; exact 1997-vs-1998 wave and individual pedigree not resolved.

She is documented among the dams that bred on in Australia and helped found the herd now run as Westholme by the Australian Agricultural Company. Her individual pedigree is not published.
notes: Fujiyoshi lean inferred from 'Fuji' element; not confirmed. Named among Westholme dams by Wagyu International.

prefecture: Sourced via ET Japan Co., Hokkaido

bio: Yuriyuho (rendered Yuriyuhoi in some listings) is one of the named foundation dams of Chris Walker's Westholme importation, the roughly 84 black females brought from ET Japan Company in Hokkaido during 1997-1998 — the largest export pool of Japanese Wagyu females before the ban. The consignment carried about 44 Fujiyoshi, 28 Kedaka (eleven Hongen-registered) and 12 Tajiri females, matched with the bulls Hirashige-Tayasu, Itomoritaka and Kitateruyasu Doi. Yuriyuho is documented among the dams that bred on in Australia and helped establish the herd now operated as Westholme by the Australian Agricultural Company. Her individual strain and pedigree are not published in the accessible sources.
notes: Spelled Yuriyuho / Yuriyuhoi across sources. Named among Westholme dams by Wagyu International; individual pedigree not found.

name: Chiyotake
bloodline_detail: Foundation cow of one of Shogo Takeda's Takeda Farm maternal lines (Sakae 2 - Chiyotake - Chiyotake 5). Takeda's herd is heavily Fujiyoshi/Itozakura-influenced via the sire Itomichi 1/2. Imported to Australia (Sumo Wagyu) as embryos from Takeda's consignment.
importer: Sumo Wagyu (Simon Coates), Australia — embryos from Shogo Takeda Farm
notable_offspring: Founder of the Chiyotake cow family; daughters Chiyotake 3 and Chiyotake 5, then Chiyotake 8 and Chiyotake 9 (sired by Itomichi). A cornerstone family of the Sumo Wagyu herd in Australia.
marbling_note: The Chiyotake line is one of Takeda Farm's elite maternal families, associated with the deep marbling that made Takeda genetics globally sought after.
bio: Chiyotake is the foundation dam of one of the six Takeda Farm cow families that Simon Coates of Sumo Wagyu brought to Australia as embryos (56 calves) from Shogo Takeda's celebrated Hokkaido herd. In Takeda's own maternal-line records she sits in the sequence Sakae 2 - Chiyotake - Chiyotake 5. Her daughters Chiyotake 3 and Chiyotake 5 each produced a daughter (Chiyotake 8 and Chiyotake 9) sired by the great Takeda bull Itomichi, and the family became a cornerstone of the Sumo Wagyu program and, through it, of Australian Wagyu breeding. Because she entered as embryos rather than as a live import, her exact birth data and registration are not published, but her line is unusually well attested for a Takeda female.
notes: Takeda maternal line per Y2 Wagyu; six Takeda cow families to Sumo per Sumo Wagyu. Embryo import, not a live foundation cow; strain lean (Fujiyoshi/Itozakura) inferred from Takeda herd composition.

name: Hikohime

Imported to Australia (Sumo Wagyu) as embryos from Takeda's consignment. Strain within Takeda's Fujiyoshi/Kedaka-influenced herd not separately documented.
importer: Sumo Wagyu (Simon Coates), Australia — embryos from Shogo Takeda Farm
notable_offspring: Founder of the Hikohime cow family within the Sumo Wagyu Australian herd.
marbling_note: Part of Takeda Farm's elite maternal base, the source of the deep-marbling reputation of Takeda genetics.
bio: Hikohime is the foundation dam of one of the six Takeda Farm cow families that Simon Coates of Sumo Wagyu imported to Australia as embryos from Shogo Takeda's Hokkaido herd. In Takeda's maternal-line records she appears in the long Kinto-descended sequence Kinto - Ezokinto - Kinto - Hikokura - Hikohime - Takechiyo - Namihikokura. Sumo Wagyu names Hikohime explicitly as one of its founding Takeda cow families, whose descendants became some of the most prized progeny of the Australian Wagyu herd. Because she was imported as embryos rather than a live cow, her exact birth data, registration and strain are not published in the accessible sources. She matters as a documented Takeda foundation dam behind the Sumo/Australian herd.
notes: Named as a founding Takeda cow family by Sumo Wagyu; maternal-line string per Y2 Wagyu. Embryo import, not a live foundation cow.

bio: Dai 2 Kinntou (also rendered Dai 2 Kinto) is the foundation dam of the Kinto cow family, one of the six Takeda Farm families that Simon Coates of Sumo Wagyu imported to Australia as embryos from Shogo Takeda's Hokkaido herd.
